next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 8

At 5:18 a.m., the next unknown number finally came. Beau said, "Get down to Legal Aid. Don't wait for coffee."

She was halfway into yesterday's jeans when he added, "It's Ava."

The line went dead before she could ask anything else.

The Mercer Public Defense Annex sat two blocks off the square in a one-story brick building that used to be a feed office. By the time Lena turned onto Willow, the street was already clogged with cruisers, church vans, and people who had come in house shoes because a cousin had texted them one sentence and they had not stopped to get dressed. Yellow tape stretched from an oak tree to a parking meter, then bowed inward where shoulders kept hitting it.

A woman in a denim robe screamed, "She got my boy out when nobody else answered the phone," and slammed both palms against a deputy's chest. He staggered, caught himself, and looked twenty years old.

Lena parked three blocks out because she had no county placard anymore. Suspension meant she had no lane, no clipped nod from uniforms, no right to cross tape. It also meant nobody had the authority to stop her from walking through the middle of people who knew her name and had reasons to hate what it represented.

"Detective Waller." Sergeant Nia Tolliver stepped in front of her with both hands up, then rubbed her thumb against a strip of pink athletic tape she kept wrapped around her index finger. Lena remembered the finger from softball season years back; Nia had dislocated it twice and never took the tape off entirely. "You're still on paper. I can't let you in unless command clears...
open_hooks_to_carry: The archive entry is planned with dread, not action bravado.
Historical documents tie deaths to property dispossession.
prev_tail_excerpt:
...n the corner of the legal pad and hated the superstition that facts got lighter once ink touched paper.

Her phone lit again with Marcus's name.

`Did you hear me tonight?`

She stared at the text long enough for the screen to dim.

`Yeah,` she wrote. `I heard you.`

Three minutes passed before another text arrived.

`Then choose us.`

Lena set the phone beside the notebook and pressed her thumb into the old knee scar through her jeans, a pressure trick the physical therapist taught her years ago when panic tried to run her body faster than sense. She could still smell Ida's kitchen on her shirt, fried onion and pepper and dish soap, the smell of being held inside a family whether you deserved it that day or not.

She opened a new message to Mara.

`I hit a wall. Book 92 pages for GT-09-31 and GT-12-44 are pulled for "audit," and the only company trail is a shared PO box with two Pike vendors. I need your archive access and your chancery source tonight or first light. County is burying the owner names.`

Mara answered in under a minute.

`You finally sound certain. Call me.`

Lena did not call right away. She looked once more at Marcus's last text, then at Jalen's geometry notebook she had brought home by reflex when she helped Ida clear the table, pages full of clean angles and one margin full of tiny drawn basketballs. He was a child trying to make a court behave like lines on paper.

She picked up the phone and called Mara.

They burned seven minutes arguing source terms and risk before Mara said she'd text coordinates when she had eyes on a safe door and a blind camera.
state_deltas_required: Access sealed chancery materials before transfer and verify land-theft linkage.
They obtain coroner packets and redacted heir lists bearing Deputy Waller's signature.
